retrieved_docs.title: Fat Embolism -- History and Physical -- General Appearance
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with FES are anxious, agitated, and ill-looking. Respiratory system Assess for abnormal breath sounds, work of breathing, and evidence of respiratory distress or impending respiratory failure.

retrieved_docs.title: Fat Embolism -- Evaluation -- Lindeque Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Lastly, Lindegue et al suggested using respiratory symptoms alone as the diagnostic criteria for FES. This criterion has not gained worldwide acceptance compared to the Gurd, Wilson, and Schoenfeld criteria. Sustained Pa02 less than 8 kilopascal Sustained PC02 greater than 7.3 kilopascal Sustained respiratory rate greater than 35 breaths per minute despite sedation Dyspnea, increased work of breathing, anxiety, tachycardia

retrieved_docs.title: Fat Embolism -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The normal partial pressure of oxygen in arterial blood is between 75 to 100mmHg. PA02 - Pa02 = 100 mmHg - 75 mm = 25 mmHg PA02-Pa02 =100 mmHg -100 mmHg = 0 mmHg This implies that the A-a gradient can have values that range from 0 to 25 mmHg The normal A-a gradient is usually less than 10 mmHg. However, a ventilation-perfusion mismatch can significantly increase FES.

retrieved_docs.title: Fat Embolism -- Treatment / Management -- Supportive Care
retrieved_docs.content: Once a patient develops FES, this is the mainstay treatment. Supportive care is geared toward adequately oxygenating the end organs.

retrieved_docs.title: Fat Embolism -- Evaluation -- Bronchoalveolar Lavage
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoalveolar lavage has been extensively researched as a diagnostic tool for FES. Lipid inclusion in the macrophages might point to a diagnosis of FES but is not specific, as this can be seen in other clinical conditions. Moreover, the procedure is time-consuming and invasive and might not give the best diagnostic yield.

retrieved_docs.title: Fat Embolism -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with traumatic FES, the prognosis depends on early open reduction and internal fixation of the long bone fracture. Most patients with adequate support therapy can recover from the neurological, respiratory, and retinal changes associated with FES. The most recent studies approximate mortality between 7% to 10%. The most common causes of morbidity or mortality include acute respiratory distress syndrome, ARDS, and cerebral edema.

retrieved_docs.title: Fat Embolism -- Etiology -- Traumatic Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatic causes of FES are more common than nontraumatic causes. Trauma as a cause of FES can occur from the fracture of the long bones, such as the femur and tibia, and also the pelvis. Operations such as pelvis or knee arthroplasty and intramedullary nailing and reaming can cause FES. The technique of inserting the intramedullary nails that can increase the likelihood of development of FES includes increased velocity in reaming, overzealousness in the nailing of the medullary cavity, and the widened gap between the nail and the cortex of the bone Other rare traumatic conditions that can cause FES include the following: Massive soft tissue damage Crush injury Prolonged cardiopulmonary resuscitation Severe burn involving more than 50% of body surface area Bone marrow transplantation Liposuction Median sternotomy

retrieved_docs.title: Fat Embolism -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional team is best able to diagnose and manage fat embolism. The diagnosis is not always simple, and there is no specific treatment for the disorder. The key is keeping the patient hydrated and promptly immobilizing or fixing the fractured limb. Many agents have been recommended for treating fat embolism, but none have proven reliable or consistently effective. A meta-analysis of seven randomized control trials using corticosteroid prophylaxis showed a close to 77% reduction in the risk for FES in a patient with a long bone fracture. There is, however, no difference in mortality, infection, or avascular necrosis between the treatment group and the control group. For this reason, the use of corticosteroids is still very controversial. The prognosis of patients with fat embolism depends on early open reduction and internal fixation of the long bone fracture. Most patients with adequate support therapy can recover from the neurological, respiratory, and retinal changes associated with FES. Delays in treatment can lead to ARDs, cerebral edema, and a mortality rate that averages 7%. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of thorax, representing a specific thoracic intervention used to treat conditions such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. By placing a closed drainage system within the thoracic cavity, clinicians can evacuate air, fluid, or blood, thereby re‑expanding the lung and relieving the pathological processes associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure exemplifies how targeted thoracic techniques are applied to manage distinct intrathoracic disorders, highlighting the interrelationship between procedural categories (subdivision of thorax) and the diseases they address.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: The mediastinum, a central subdivision of the thorax, serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower respiratory diseases such as lung and mediastinal abscesses (خراج) and is implicated in mediastinal disorders (اضطراب منصفي) including effusions. Mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية)—encompassing mid‑mediastinal, posterior‑mediastinal, superior‑mediastinal, and calcified variants—along with mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (unilateral right, unilateral left, and bilateral) are key thoracic pathologies. Chest computed tomography (CT), classified under imaging procedures related to the chest and respiratory tract, is the primary diagnostic modality for visualizing these conditions. Standard chest CT, thoracic CT without contrast, and specialized thoracic CT scans (e.g., “thoracic CT scan: mediastinal‑pleural fistula”) function as parent procedures that generate specific findings such as “computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass,” “mediastinal mass containing calcium,” “mass of posterior mediastinum,” “mass of superior mediastinum,” and “mediastinal effusion.” The chest CT hierarchy also includes pulmonary angiographic imaging as a child study. Closed chest drainage is an interventional procedure linked to the thoracic subdivision and guided by chest CT imaging. Breast CT, while primarily a breast imaging study, is positioned inversely to radiographic breast imaging and is subsumed under the broader chest CT category, reflecting overlap between thoracic and breast imaging. Overall, the network emphasizes the mediastinum as a pivotal structure in lower respiratory disease, abscess formation, mass lesions, and fistulous complications, with chest CT—particularly non‑contrast thoracic protocols—serving as the essential tool for diagnosis and procedural planning.

Collectively, the knowledge base emphasizes how genetic, infectious, hematologic, respiratory, neurological, and psychiatric factors intersect with critical outcomes such as respiratory failure, hypoxemia, syncope, suffocation, femoral neck fracture, and death, and underscores the importance of both therapeutic interventions and prevention‑control strategies in patient care.
